did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning image recognition models (specifically Inception v3, a convolutional neural network, implemented via TensorFlow) to differentiate between melanoma and basal cell carcinoma in dermatological images of varying Fitzpatrick skin types.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as a 'Short Paper' and contains original experimental work including an Abstract with Background, Objective, Methods, Results, and Conclusions. The Methods section describes training, validating, and testing two deep learning models (Inception v3) on specific datasets of dermatological images. The Results section reports new statistical metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC, etc.) derived from these experiments, indicating it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: Performance of Artificial Intelligence Imaging Models in Detecting Dermatological Manifestations in Higher Fitzpatrick Skin Color Classifications
brief_summary: This study evaluated whether deep learning image recognition models perform differently when differentiating between melanoma and basal cell carcinoma in individuals with darker skin (Fitzpatrick types 4 and 5) compared to lighter skin (types 1, 2, and 3). The researchers trained, validated, and tested two Inception v3 models using open-source images acquired from the Hellenic Dermatological Atlas, Dermatology Atlas, Interactive Dermatology Atlas, and DermNet NZ. Each model was trained on an equal number of images (150) for its respective skin type group. The main evaluation approach involved calculating sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative predictive values, F1 scores, and area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUC) curves. The results showed that the model trained on light skin images achieved higher performance metrics (e.g., sensitivity of 0.60 for melanoma) than the model trained on skin of color (sensitivity of 0.53). The study concluded that a larger number of images of dermatological diseases in individuals with darker skin color is needed for artificial intelligence models to perform equally well.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes differentiated by the deep learning models. Used in training (75 images per model group), validation, and testing. Specifically evaluated for sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, F1 score, and AUC.

all_task_types.target: Differentiation between melanoma and basal cell carcinoma (binary classification)
all_task_types.details: The study trained Inception v3 models to differentiate between two discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. The task is binary classification, evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), negative predictive value (NPV), F1 score, and area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUC) curves. The model outputs probabilities for each class to determine the predicted category.

conclusions: When trained on an equal number of images, the artificial intelligence model provided with images of melanoma and basal cell carcinoma from individuals with light skin color (Fitzpatrick types 1, 2, and 3) performed better than the model provided with images from individuals with darker skin color (Fitzpatrick types 4 and 5).

The model trained on light skin images achieved higher sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, and F1 score compared to the model trained on skin of color images.
The average area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUC) curve for the light skin color models was 0.598, compared to 0.500 for the skin of color models.

The reduced performance of the skin of color model may be attributed to the limited number of available images for Fitzpatrick skin types 4 and 5 and potentially greater variability in the presentation of dermatological diseases in darker-skinned individuals.

A higher number of images of dermatological diseases in individuals with darker skin color than in individuals with light skin color would need to be gathered for artificial intelligence models to perform equally well.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a very small sample size (150 images per cohort for training), which the authors acknowledge as a constraint that may affect the consistency of results.

There is a significant population/dataset bias: the 'skin of color' cohort (Fitzpatrick types 4 and 5) suffered from a lack of available images, leading to inferior model performance compared to the light skin cohort.
The models demonstrated weak discriminatory ability, with average AUCs of only 0.598 (light skin) and 0.500 (skin of color), indicating performance close to random chance.

No external validation was performed; the models were trained, validated, and tested on images from the same four open-source datasets (Hellenic Dermatological Atlas, Dermatology Atlas, Interactive Dermatology Atlas, and DermNet NZ).
The study did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying solely on descriptive performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, F1 score, AUC).

Potential data leakage or bias exists as the training data was sourced entirely from open-access atlases, which may not represent the true clinical distribution of dermatological manifestations in darker skin types.
The authors suggest that the lower performance in skin of color may be due to both data scarcity and greater variability in disease presentation, which the small sample size fails to capture.
